figures.gloss: A young merchant of Shiraz who in May 1844 declared himself the Báb — the Gate — first as channel of the Hidden Twelfth Imam awaited by Shi'a Islam, then as the Qá'im himself and an independent Manifestation of God bearing a new law. He was executed by a firing squad of 750 rifles in Tabriz in 1850, aged thirty.
figures.facet: He shows a messianic claim escalating in public, in stages, under interrogation — from gate, to Imam, to theophany — each step preserved in trial records; Shi'i expectation raised to such a pitch that the herald became the revelation.
figures.provenance: Qayyúm al-Asmá (1844); the Persian Bayán; Abbas Amanat, 'Resurrection and Renewal' (1989); Denis MacEoin, 'The Messiah of Shiraz' (2009).

figures.gloss: The Báb's book of law, composed in the prison-fortress of Máku (c. 1847–48) in Persian and Arabic: a deliberately unfinished code built on the number nineteen — a calendar of nineteen months of nineteen days, new tithes, a new qibla — abrogating the Qur'anic dispensation.
figures.facet: It is a scripture designed to expire: the Bayán repeatedly subordinates its own authority to 'Him Whom God shall make manifest' — a law-book engineered to hand itself over.
figures.provenance: The Persian Bayán; the Arabic Bayán; E. G. Browne's editions and notices; Amanat (1989).

figures.gloss: Fátimih Baraghání of Qazvin, poet and Shaykhi scholar, the one woman among the Letters of the Living. At the Badasht conference of 1848 she appeared before the assembled Bábís unveiled, announcing that the old law was ended; she was strangled in Tehran in 1852 during the anti-Bábí pogrom.
figures.facet: Her unveiling is the movement's rupture made flesh — the abrogation of the shari'a performed as a single gesture on a woman's face, decades before any organized movement for women's emancipation in Iran.
figures.provenance: Nabíl's Narrative (The Dawn-Breakers); Abbas Amanat, 'Resurrection and Renewal' (1989); her surviving Persian poems.

figures.gloss: The first eighteen believers who independently recognized the Báb in 1844 and who, with him, complete the sacred unit of nineteen — the first Váhid (Unity) of the new creation, dispatched across Persia and Iraq as its apostles.
figures.facet: Bábí doctrine reads them as raj'a, the return: the Fourteen Immaculates of Shi'ism and the four original Gates come back as living men and one woman — the old sacred cosmos reincarnated as the new community's founding cell.
figures.provenance: The Persian Bayán; Nabíl's Narrative; MacEoin, 'The Messiah of Shiraz' (2009).

figures.gloss: The greater revelation the Bayán ceaselessly points toward — a coming theophany whose recognition outweighs all Bábí law, and to whom the Báb commends his community and his book.
figures.facet: It converts the religion into a fuse: within thirteen years of the Báb's death, Bahá'u'lláh's 1863 claim to be this figure absorbed nearly the whole community, while Subh-i-Azal's rejection of that claim left Azalí Bábism a dwindling remnant.
figures.provenance: The Persian Bayán; Bahá'u'lláh's Kitáb-i-Badí'; MacEoin (2009).

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: The same historical person — Bábism's founding prophet as canonized inside its child religion; parent and child share one hinge figure.
GOLD SEAM: The explicit parent-link — Bahá'u'lláh's 1863 declaration claims this precise Bábí office, turning Bábism's central promise into the Bahá'í Faith's foundation.
GOLD SEAM: The Báb's initial 1844 claim was to be the gate of the Hidden Twelfth Imam — Twelver Shi'i expectation, sharpened by the Shaykhi school, is the soil the movement grew from.
GOLD SEAM: The Bayán is modeled on the Qur'an's station while explicitly claiming to abrogate its dispensation — successor scripture, not commentary.
GOLD SEAM: 'The Gate' as a standing office of esoteric mediation in Shi'i-derived systems — the threshold between the hidden divine and the community.
Intra-tradition: Táhirih is the one woman among the Letters, accepted into their number without ever meeting the Báb in person.
Intra-tradition: the Bayán's law is explicitly provisional, held in trust for Him Whom God shall make manifest.

dossier.essence: A messianic religion born inside Twelver Shi'i Iran when Siyyid 'Alí-Muhammad of Shiraz, a young merchant schooled in Shaykhi expectation, declared himself the Báb ('the Gate') to Mullá Husayn on the night of 22–23 May 1844 — a claim that escalated publicly from gate of the Hidden Imam, to the Qá'im himself, to an independent Manifestation of God bearing a new law. The movement swept Qajar Persia, fought sieges at Fort Tabarsí, Nayríz, and Zanján (1848–50), and was broken by the Báb's execution at Tabriz in 1850 and the pogrom of 1852. By the 1860s the great majority of Bábís followed Bahá'u'lláh into the Bahá'í Faith; Bábism survives only as a small Azalí remnant.
dossier.cosmos: Reality unfolds as a succession of prophetic cycles, each opened by a Manifestation of God whose revelation abrogates the last — the Báb's dispensation superseding the Qur'anic one as Islam superseded Christianity. The Bayán reorganizes the sacred cosmos around the number nineteen (a calendar of nineteen months of nineteen days, the founding unit of eighteen Letters of the Living plus the Báb), and reinterprets resurrection, paradise, and hell as the recognition or rejection of each new theophany rather than as literal afterlife geography. The whole system points forward: its center of gravity is 'Him Whom God shall make manifest', the greater revelation to come.
dossier.practice: The Bayán legislated a new religious order — the badí' calendar, new tithes and pilgrimage, a qibla oriented on the Báb himself — but the movement was destroyed before most of it could be institutionalized; lived Bábism was recognition, the copying and circulation of the Báb's torrential writings, missionary dispatch of the Letters of the Living, and, under siege, armed defense. Táhirih's unveiling at Badasht (1848) enacted the abrogation of the shari'a in a single gesture. The Azalí remnant has survived largely through quietism and dissimulation.
dossier.canon: The Qayyúm al-Asmá (1844), the Persian and Arabic Bayán (c. 1847–48), and a vast corpus of the Báb's further tablets and prayers; Nabíl's narrative 'The Dawn-Breakers' is the classic (Bahá'í) chronicle of the period.
dossier.reflects: Millenarian expectation consuming its own fulfillment — a religion deliberately built as a fuse, whose scripture subordinates itself to a successor it does not name.
sources: Abbas Amanat, 'Resurrection and Renewal: The Making of the Babi Movement in Iran, 1844–1850' (1989)
Denis MacEoin, 'The Messiah of Shiraz: Studies in Early and Middle Babism' (2009)
Nabíl-i-A'zam (Muhammad-i-Zarandí), 'The Dawn-Breakers: Nabíl's Narrative of the Early Days of the Bahá'í Revelation' (trans. Shoghi Effendi, 1932)
Edward G. Browne (ed. and trans.), 'A Traveller's Narrative Written to Illustrate the Episode of the Báb' (1891)
